summary: Ed Stanfield was the last state field representative before the Southern Regional Council stopped having one. He left to take a job in Texas after some clashes with Paul over how the state council director should operate. Ed was a bit dogmatic about following a grand pattern for the councils, which led to heated disagreements, especially with Alice Spearman in South Carolina, and eventually to his resignation.

Paul was involved in the Southern Regional Council and had some disagreements with Ed Stanfield about how the state council directors should conduct their work. Their differing views on the role and structure of the state councils caused tension, particularly with Ed's insistence on a strict pattern. Paul was part of the leadership during the time when the state councils were being organized and funded in the 1950s.

Alice Spearman was the director of the South Carolina Council and married to Marion Wright, a former vice-president and president of the Southern Regional Council. She was very active in her community and ran a successful program with plenty of funding. Alice had a strong practical approach, saving money quietly, and was involved during the early years of the state councils in the 1950s.

Marion Wright was a former vice-president and president of the Southern Regional Council when I first joined. He was married to Alice Spearman, who directed the South Carolina Council. Marion had retired by the time of the events I recall, but he played a leadership role in the Council's earlier years.

Tom was a bright young man who came in as the new director after Alice Spearman's tenure at the South Carolina Council. Although I never met him personally, I heard he took over when Alice had saved up money that the board didn't know about. He represented a new generation of leadership in the state councils after the early 1950s.

The Southern Regional Council was the central organization overseeing the state councils across the South starting in the early 1950s. We coordinated funding, hired directors, and held annual meetings and institutes for state council directors. The Council also worked closely with foundations like Ford and Rockefeller to support civil rights and integration efforts in southern states during the 1950s and 1960s.

Texas came up because Ed Stanfield took a job there after leaving his position as state field representative. It was notable since Texas was part of the Southern Regional Council's area of influence, but Ed moved there, marking the end of his role with us. Texas was one of the southern states involved in the Council's activities during the 1950s.

South Carolina had an active state council led by Alice Spearman in the 1950s, who was very effective and practical in managing funds. The state council there was one of the more successful ones, even securing foundation grants later on. South Carolina's council was involved in community work and had a reputation for doing good despite some internal conflicts early on.

Oklahoma was unique among the southern states because it refused to be called a southern state and declined to set up a state council. This was interesting since Oklahoma was part of the region but chose not to participate in the Southern Regional Council's state council program in the 1950s. I grew up there and found it notable that they didn't want to be grouped with the other southern states.

Louisiana was a tough state for the Southern Regional Council's efforts. In New Orleans, the state council never had any black staff members, reflecting the difficult climate there. For about eight years, there was almost no activity in Louisiana's council until a black woman contractor in Baton Rouge tried to revive it, but she faced many challenges and was unable to make much progress.

New Orleans was part of Louisiana's state council area, but it was very difficult to have integrated staff there in the 1950s. The council never had any black employees in New Orleans, which showed how resistant the city was to integration efforts. A young man worked there briefly, but the overall environment was not supportive of the council's goals.

Mississippi's state council was almost a secret organization in the 1950s, with very little publicity and a black woman director at the start whose name I can't recall. Despite the secrecy, the council managed to get grants from the Rockefeller Foundation and later the Ford Foundation. Ken Dean was a notable director who worked actively with voter registration and school integration efforts in Jackson, Mississippi, during the 1960s.

Georgia's state council was based in Atlanta and initially did not have a mixed staff for a long time. They did hire black consultants from Atlanta University to assist with their work. John McCowan became the first black director of the Georgia Council around 1966 or 1967, marking a significant step toward integration within the council's leadership.

Atlanta University provided black consultants who worked with the Georgia state council. Although the council itself did not have integrated staff for a long time, these consultants contributed valuable expertise. This collaboration helped the council stay connected with the black academic community in Georgia during the civil rights era.

John McCowan was a significant figure who became the assistant director in Arkansas and later the director of the Georgia Council around 1966 or 1967. His leadership represented progress in integrating the state councils. He was one of the few black directors in the Southern Regional Council's network during that period.

Arkansas had a state council where John McCowan served as assistant director before moving to Georgia. Elisha Coleman was also involved and eventually became the director. The Arkansas Council was considered healthy and effective, even securing federal funds for its programs.

Elisha Coleman was the assistant director in Arkansas and later became the director of the Arkansas state council. He took over after the previous white director retired due to age and health. Under his leadership, the council was quite effective and managed to secure federal funding for its work.

Virginia's state council was supported by a minister named Jack Marion, who was very effective at fundraising. He secured many one-hundred-dollar memberships and built the council's financial base well. However, after he left for another job, the council's momentum declined because his successor couldn't maintain the same level of support.

Jack Marion was a minister in Virginia who played a key role in building the state's council membership and financial support. He raised numerous one-hundred-dollar memberships and really strengthened the council's resources. Unfortunately, when he left for another job, the council couldn't sustain the same level of activity and funding.

The Tennessee state council struggled financially for many years and only recently began to receive foundation grants. Leslie Dunbar funded Tennessee's council with a $25,000 grant, helping to support its activities. The council's work varied, but like others, it aimed to ease racial tensions and support civil rights efforts in the state.

The Rockefeller Foundation provided grants to some state councils, including Mississippi, to help support their civil rights work. These grants were separate from the Southern Regional Council's main funding and were important for sustaining council activities. The foundation's support helped councils pursue voter registration and integration efforts during the 1950s and 1960s.

The Ford Foundation gave grants starting in 1965 to support the state councils, although the amounts were not enough to fully sustain them. The Southern Regional Council received these funds and distributed them to the councils, but the funding declined over three years and ended recently. The Ford Foundation's support was critical during the mid-1960s for the councils' operations.

Leslie Dunbar was a private funder who supported several state councils independently of the Southern Regional Council. He gave grants of $25,000 to Mississippi and Tennessee and about $14,000 to South Carolina. Dunbar had faith in leaders like Ken Dean in Mississippi and maintained a close relationship with them, often visiting and discussing their work in New York.
cues: grants to Mississippi, Tennessee, South Carolina

summary: The Field Foundation was another source of funding for some state councils, separate from the Southern Regional Council's main grants. Leslie Dunbar, who was associated with the Field Foundation, provided financial support to Mississippi, Tennessee, and South Carolina councils. This foundation's grants helped sustain council activities when other funding was declining.

Ken Dean was a very effective director of the Mississippi state council who worked actively on voter registration and school integration. He was vocal and dedicated, working year-round with young people and civil rights activists. Ken hired black secretaries and maintained the council's office in the First Federal Building in Jackson. He left recently to study at Syracuse University and married a Tennessee woman while in Mississippi.

Jackson was the location of the Mississippi state council's office, where Ken Dean worked with a staff that included black secretaries. The city was a hub for voter registration and integration efforts supported by the council during the 1960s. Despite the dangers and difficulties, the council maintained a presence there to support civil rights work.

Syracuse University is where Ken Dean went to study after leaving his position as director of the Mississippi state council. His move there marked a transition from active field work to further education. This university became part of his personal and professional development following his civil rights activism.

Gatlinburg was the site of a significant meeting in September 1967 where state council directors and Southern Regional Council staff gathered for a week. At this meeting, the state councils debated resolutions against the Vietnam War and discussed the Council's stance on political issues. It was a time of tension and differing views among young leaders about the role of the councils in political activism.

The Vietnam War was a contentious issue among the state councils and the Southern Regional Council in the late 1960s. At the 1967 Gatlinburg meeting, many state councils wanted the Southern Regional Council to take a public stand against the war. This caused heated discussions as some thought it was not the right time to address such political matters while focusing on civil rights work.

George Wallace was mentioned as a figure associated with Alabama's reputation during the civil rights era. People often compared Alabama and Mississippi, sometimes preferring to live in parts of Mississippi rather than Alabama because of Wallace's influence and other issues. His name came up in discussions about the difficulties faced in southern states during that time.
